ai_peak.text: AI valuations show acceleration similar to late 1999, suggesting continued gains before a potential peak in 2027. The analogy breaks down if AI delivers transformative products that justify current prices, unlike dot-com's mostly vaporous promises.

buffett.text:
Market cap to GDP has grown 40% since ChatGPT's launch, tracking the pattern seen during dot-com's rise phase. The metric suggests AI is roughly where dot-com was three years before its peak, though compressed timelines mean nothing about actual outcomes.

Caveat: Market cap to GDP can rise from genuine productivity gains, not just speculation, so historical pattern-matching tells you little about whether this boom has real foundations.

capex.text: AI capex is climbing steeply but hasn't yet matched the frenzied dot-com spending peak. The analogy suggests the surge could crest in mid-2027 if historical compression holds. Caveat: AI infrastructure may sustain higher spending longer than dot-com did, since actual revenue-generating services now exist.

ipo_froth.text: IPO enthusiasm shows similar early-stage dynamics to the dot-com boom, with first-day pops still modest relative to peak froth. The pattern suggests room for further euphoria before any potential reversal. Caveat: IPO metrics lag actual market peaks, so this analogy may understate how far sentiment has already advanced.

it_invest.text: IT investment share rose sharply in both eras, with AI now at roughly two-thirds of dot-com peak intensity. The acceleration phase matches 1999's early surge, suggesting similar structural shifts in capital allocation. Caveat: investment patterns can diverge from valuation bubbles, so matching spending trajectories doesn't guarantee matching crashes.

margin_debt.text: Margin debt shows similar acceleration patterns in both eras, with AI leverage currently climbing at a pace that mirrors dot-com's mid-rise phase. The comparison suggests shared investor confidence, though leverage remains well below prior extremes. Caveat: debt behavior could diverge sharply if AI monetization proves more durable than dot-com business models.

market_concentration.text: AI market concentration is rising steeply but remains well below dot-com extremes, suggesting earlier bubble stages. The compression ratio implies a faster timeline, but concentration alone cannot predict peaks, only describe current structure.

monetary.text: Monetary and sentiment conditions today are much weaker than they were at the dot-com peak, suggesting less exuberance. A skeptical reader should remember that sentiment can shift rapidly and dramatically without warning.

nasdaq.text: The Nasdaq has risen 2.4x since ChatGPT, matching dot-com's acceleration phase about 10 months before the 2000 peak. If the timeline compresses similarly, the analogy suggests a top around mid-2027. Caveat: index composition differs vastly, and today's mega-cap tech firms have real earnings, unlike many dot-com companies.

price_appreciation.text: AI valuations are accelerating at a pace similar to late 1998, suggesting the boom phase still has runway. Caveat: compressed timelines may reflect faster information spread rather than fundamentally different bubble dynamics.

semis.text: Semiconductor production lacks the clear boom-and-bust pattern that defines the dot-com bubble, making it a poor analog for predicting AI's trajectory. The metric shows sustained growth across both eras rather than the speculative spike and crash needed for meaningful comparison. Caveat: industries with genuine secular demand (chips for computing) behave fundamentally differently from those driven primarily by speculative excess.

sentiment.text:
Consumer sentiment shows no sign of following the dot-com pattern, remaining flat instead of building toward the euphoric peaks that preceded that bubble's burst. This suggests either fundamentally different market psychology or that sentiment may not be a reliable indicator for this cycle.

Caveat: Consumer sentiment data quality and methodology have evolved significantly since the 1990s, making direct comparison imperfect.

sp500.text: The S&P 500 has risen sharply since ChatGPT's launch, tracking a similar acceleration phase to the dot-com bubble's 1998 advance. If the current compression holds, the broad market could peak around early 2028. Caveat: stock indexes reflect many sectors beyond AI, so this analogy conflates bubble dynamics in one industry with economy-wide valuation.

speculation.text: Speculative fervor in AI is rising sharply but remains well below dot-com extremes, placing the market in a mid-1990s acceleration phase by analogy. Caveat: compressed timelines assume AI bubble dynamics will mirror dot-com ones, which ignores structural differences in modern markets.

tech_leadership.text: Tech leadership concentration has risen sharply since ChatGPT, tracking the dot-com pattern at a compressed pace. The analogy suggests a potential peak around late 2026, though this assumes structural similarities that may not hold. Caveat: the metric measures leadership only, not whether the underlying AI investments prove economically valuable.

tech_orders.text:
Equipment orders show AI accelerating much as the dot-com sector did before 2000, but AI remains well below peak dot-com intensity. The parallel suggests sustained growth ahead, though past bubbles have surprised with speed and severity.

Caveat: Equipment orders reflect business confidence, which can reverse sharply if sentiment shifts faster than this metric suggests.

valuation.text: AI valuations are rising fast, though still below dot-com peak intensity. The pattern suggests continued gains ahead, but sector concentration and fundamental differences in profitability make valuation comparisons inherently uncertain.

valuation_multiple.text: Valuations have inflated to levels last seen in late 1999, suggesting the AI boom could follow the dot-com trajectory with a potential peak roughly two years out. Caveat: valuations alone don't determine crashes, which depend on earnings growth, rates, and sentiment shifts that may diverge from the 2000 playbook.

yield_curve.text: The yield curve hasn't tightened as the AI era matured, unlike the dot-com period when it compressed sharply into the peak. This divergence suggests either different economic conditions or that this particular metric simply doesn't track the current cycle. Skeptics should remember that yield curves respond to Fed policy and inflation expectations, not just speculative excess.
